attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic for her first antenatal visit. She is eight weeks pregnant with her first child. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. During the consultation, she asks about dietary recommendations during pregnancy, specifically mentioning that she enjoys eating fish. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding fish consumption during pregnancy?
options: Continue eating all types of fish without restriction
Limit consumption of all fish to once per month
Avoid all fish due to mercury risk
Increase consumption of all types of fish for omega-3 benefits
Avoid high-mercury fish, but consume low-mercury fish 2-3 times per week
attributes.explanation:
The most appropriate advice for pregnant women regarding fish consumption is to avoid high-mercury fish, but consume low-mercury fish 2-3 times per week. This balanced approach allows pregnant women to benefit from the nutritional value of fish while minimising the risk of mercury exposure to the developing fetus.

Avoiding all fish due to mercury risk is overly restrictive and unnecessary. Fish is an excellent source of protein, omega-3 fatty acids, and other nutrients that are beneficial for fetal development. Completely eliminating fish from the diet could deprive the mother and fetus of these important nutrients.

Limiting consumption of all fish to once per month is also too restrictive. Most fish are low in mercury and safe to eat more frequently during pregnancy. The benefits of eating fish generally outweigh the risks when consumed in moderation.

Continuing to eat all types of fish without restriction is not advisable, as some fish contain high levels of mercury which can be harmful to fetal development. Pregnant women should be aware of which fish to avoid or limit.

Increasing consumption of all types of fish for omega-3 benefits is not recommended, as it does not account for the mercury content in different fish species. While omega-3 fatty acids are beneficial, indiscriminate increase in fish consumption could lead to excessive mercury intake.

The correct advice is to avoid high-mercury fish such as shark, swordfish, king mackerel, and tilefish. However, pregnant women should be encouraged to eat a variety of low-mercury fish 2-3 times per week. Good choices include salmon, sardines, trout, and canned light tuna. This approach provides the nutritional benefits of fish while minimising mercury exposure. It is also important to advise pregnant women to check local advisories about the safety of fish caught in their area.
learningPoints: Pregnant women should limit consumption of high-mercury fish to reduce risk of fetal neurodevelopmental effects.
Low-mercury fish are safe and beneficial during pregnancy due to their omega-3 fatty acid content.
Shark, swordfish, king mackerel, and tilefish are high in mercury and should be avoided during pregnancy.
references: Stratakis N, Conti DV, Borras E, et al. Association of Fish Consumption and Mercury Exposure During Pregnancy With Metabolic Health and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Children. JAMA Netw Open. 2020 Mar 2;3(3):e201007.

attributes.description: Suitable for a junior, pre-clinical medical student
